availability.p: Published under a Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 15 [Bnf 12926 Transcription]
div.p: Chapter 15.

¶ Up to this point we have taught that neither the Church nor Christian princes can punish pagans, even if they are liable for the most serious crimes. Now, for a complete understanding of this treatise, I will set forth six cases in which the Church can punish pagans, and I will also respond in passing to the things that Sepúlveda teaches near the end of his second argument.
Before all else, it must be supposed that the Church can exercise temporal jurisdiction over unbelievers in three ways. The third member may be seen below in chapter 37.

First, when unbelievers are subjects of the Church — both in disposition and in act — or of some member of the Church, such as a Christian prince. Unbelievers of this kind are heretics, concerning whom we have argued above in chapter 7 and will argue below. For these, having once received the Catholic truth, must be compelled to return to the fold of Christ. Unbelievers of this kind are also Jews and Moors who are subjects of Christian kings.

Over these, since they are subject in both law and fact, the Church can enact laws — which is sufficiently proven from the title on Jews and Saracens and from the title on heretics in the book of the Decretals, the Sixth, and the Clementines; and c. de iudaeis, paganis et templis eorum; and c. de hereticis et apostatis. And in the Spanish language we have very many laws promulgated by the Castilian kings concerning Jews and Moors.

And thus contentious jurisdiction can be exercised over these — that is, even against their will — as explained by the doctors in l. inter stipulantem. §. 1. ff. de verborum obligatione. For the Church does this legitimately, since they are subject in both act and disposition to Christian princes, that is, to members of the Church.

¶ Second, the Church holds only in disposition contentious jurisdiction over other unbelievers, so that it may bring this into act when the occasion arises or while the cause subsists — but not otherwise, on account of the ten reasons adduced above, and especially according to that word of Paul: "What business is it of mine to judge those who are outside?"

Now these unbelievers are those who have never professed the faith of Christ through baptism — of whatever religion, worship, rite, or sect they may be — provided they are not Jews, and have their own kingdoms separate from Christian kingdoms, so that they are not subjects of the Church or of any of its members.

We said "provided they are not Jews," because Jews, on account of the crime committed by them — killing Jesus Christ our Lord — are by right servants of the Church, as stated in c. etsi iudaeos: de iudaeis 161. "Those whom their own fault has consigned to perpetual servitude, let Christian piety receive and sustain" and so forth. But in fact it was Constantine who subjected them to servitude, according to the gloss in c. consuluit de iudeis.

Nevertheless, they are not properly slaves, nor can works be imposed on them against their will, nor should they be oppressed contrary to justice and right, as stated in c. qui sincera. 45. distinctio and in c. sicut iudaei. de iudicii. We also said that unbelievers are not subjects of the Church but have separate kingdoms — because in this respect one and the same reasoning applies to all unbelievers of whatever region or rite they may be, on the basis of the reasons set forth above. For no one will be able to assign any rational basis for a distinction.

¶ Over all unbelievers, therefore, the Church can exercise contentious jurisdiction in six cases.
Six cases in which the Church can exercise contentious jurisdiction over all unbelievers.

¶ First, if they unjustly possess kingdoms from which they have unjustly despoiled the Christian people — especially if Christians live among them. Of this kind are the Byzantine Empire, Rhodes, Hungary, Belgrade, and Africa, all of which once worshipped Christ. For since by holding those kingdoms they inflict upon us a continuous injury, the Church can bring into act the jurisdiction it holds in disposition over them, in order to recover through the compulsion of war what is its own. For no other remedy remains.

And it is about unbelievers of this kind that Hostiensis speaks in c. quod super his. de voto, and the other doctors there. And Oldradus in consilium 72. And Albericus on the Rubric of c. de paganis et eorum templis. Where Hostiensis and his followers say that unbelievers, if they do not acknowledge the lordship of the Church, are unworthy of all lordship and jurisdiction. And Hostiensis understands this of the jurisdiction and empire that infidel princes hold over lands that were once Christian, and over the Christians dwelling among them — or where the whole people or some part of the people has converted to Christ, while the prince persists still in his blindness and unbelief.

So interprets Antonius Butrius in his commentary on c. quod super of the Decretals, reconciling the opinion of Hostiensis and Innocent IV in these words: "This conclusion of Innocent is confirmed by Hostiensis, who moderates it: so that unbelievers could have jurisdiction over Christians when the unbelievers acknowledge the jurisdiction of the Church. For such persons can have, and by the Church's tolerance can hold, lordships and rights" and so forth. From which it is clearly and plainly evident that Hostiensis understood the matter to concern infidel lords who have under their power certain Christians — that they must acknowledge the lordship and jurisdiction of the Church in order to be worthy of such principality or lordship (that is) over the faithful.

And these things accord with the teaching of the holy doctor, the divine Thomas, Secunda Secundae q. 10. art. 10, that the Church, "although it could by its own right remove through sentence or decree the right of lordship or rule that infidel princes hold over Christians — because by reason of their unbelief they deserve to lose power over the faithful who are transferred into the children of God — nevertheless tolerates them in order to avoid scandal." These are the words of Saint Thomas.

And so the opinion of Hostiensis is to be understood not indiscriminately of all unbelievers, but of those who were of his time — namely when an infidel prince holds Christians under his dominion, or has usurped Christian kingdoms. For these seem to be bound to acknowledge the lordship of the Church.

But concerning others — such as our Indians, who have neither usurped Christian kingdoms nor rule over Christians, nor had ever before our age heard the name of this our world — it is absurd that they should be bound to acknowledge the lordship of the Church, or else be deprived of their principality, since they have no way to divine this without the teaching of the faith. For if the opinion of Hostiensis were understood otherwise, it would itself be heretical — as we will prove at length elsewhere — and great dangers in the world and intolerable absurdities would follow.

¶ Second, the Church actually exercises jurisdiction over pagans when in provinces that were once under Christian rule they practice idolatry, or infect a region with abominable and unspeakable vices against nature. For since in those places the true sacrifice of the body and blood of Christ has been consecrated, and pure worship has been offered to the true God, and the sacred sacraments have been administered — concerning which see Saint Thomas, Tertia Pars, q. 63, art. 2, 3, 4, 6, in c.

Whose end it is that human beings, rendering glory and honor to the true God, should subject themselves entirely to him in body and soul — according to Thomas, Secunda Secundae, q. 93, art. 27, c., and Book 3, c. 119, Contra Gentiles — it is by no means reasonable that the Church, if it has the power, should tolerate such things, since they redound to the reproach and dishonor of the Christian name.

And this no Christian ought to dissemble, as stated in c. in nonnullis. de iudaeis, where it says: "we ought not to dissemble the reproach of him who has blotted out our reproaches for us," and in l. Manichaeos c. de hereticis, it is said: "we desire that a public crime be what is committed against the divine religion: it is an injury inflicted upon all" and so forth. And "public law consists in sacred matters and in priests," as stated in l. 1. ius publicum, ff. de iustitia et iure.

This case together with the preceding one is specifically stated by Innocent in c. maiores. de baptismo, at the end, where, speaking of the Saracens, he says: "that they are not to be attacked by war by the Church, if they neither defile the land of Christians with their filth nor assail the persons of Christians" and so forth.

Innocent took that word "filth" from sacred scripture, where it chiefly signifies idolatry or another unclean abomination, especially against nature. For so we read in 3 Kings 15 of King Asa, "that he removed the effeminate men from the land and cleansed all the filth of the idols which his fathers had made." And in Apocalypse, chapter 22: "he who does harm, let him do harm still: and he who is in filth, let him be made filthy still."

And thus must be understood what the same Innocent holds in c. quod super his de voto, 1st column, How Innocent is to be understood. where he believes that "if a gentile who has no law except that of nature acts against the law of nature, he can lawfully be punished by the pope." And a little further on he says: "I say the same (he states) if they worship idols. For it is natural to worship one sole God the Creator and not creatures." So far he.

This must be supplemented and understood as follows: if the provinces where those sins are committed were once inhabited by a Christian people — otherwise more absurdities would follow than from the teaching of Hostiensis — if Innocent were to understand this absolutely of all parts of the world whose inhabitants had not yet received the faith nor had the Church been founded among them. First, because that would be contrary to what we proved above by ten effective arguments. Second, because Innocent does not prove his assertion but merely cites the fact that the Sodomites, who sinned against the law of nature, were punished by God — Genesis 14.

"Since the judgments of God (Innocent says) are examples for us, I do not see why the pope, who is the vicar of Christ, cannot do this — provided the power is available." So Innocent. Certainly, if all the judgments of God were to be imitated by us, it would follow that on account of the sin of idolatry and the sin against nature, the innocent children of those who committed such sins ought to be killed — on the grounds that God had done so.

¶ Similarly, on account of rebellion or schism against the Church, not only the rebels and schismatics would have to be killed, but also their children and wives, and all their possessions would have to be destroyed — on the grounds that in Numbers, chapter 16, we read that Korah, Dathan, and Abiron, together with their tents and all their substance, were swallowed up by the earth on account of their disobedience toward Moses and Aaron.

¶ Similarly, that on account of serious theft, not only would the thief have to be stoned, but also his children, wife, oxen, sheep, beasts of burden, and all his possessions would have to be consumed by fire — on the grounds that the Lord commanded this to be done to Achan in Joshua, chapter 7, because he had hidden a scarlet mantle and two hundred shekels taken from the plunder of the enemies which God had commanded to be burned.

¶ Similarly it would follow that if children insulted a prelate — even the supreme one — they should be thrown to the lions, since we read in 4 Kings, chapter 2, that forty-two children were torn apart by bears because they mocked the prophet Elisha as he was coming into the city of Bethel, taunting him with these words: "Go up, bald-head! Go up, bald-head!" — for which cause they were cursed by Elisha and torn apart by bears that came out of the forest.

And other terrible judgments of God which we omit for the sake of brevity — which, if they were to be imitated by us, would undoubtedly cause us to commit a great many and very serious unjust acts and gravest sins, and a thousand thousand absurdities would follow.

¶ Therefore not all the judgments of God are examples for us to imitate. Nor will anyone be able to assign any reason — except a frivolous one — why we should not imitate the judgments of God in these cases just as in that one.

To which we respond as follows. First, because examples and deeds of this kind from the Old Testament are to be marveled at rather than imitated, as was said above and will be said again. Second, because when God saw that certain sins had begun to be committed, he was accustomed to punish them very severely at the outset, so that people, frightened by that most severe example, might be deterred from similar sins. Of this kind was the destruction or submersion of Sodom.

At the beginning of the bans at the entry into the Promised Land was the punishment of Achan and the transgression of the first ban. Similarly, the punishment for the first exercise of simony was harshly inflicted upon Simony's first practitioner: it was severely punished in the case of Gehazi, who received silver and garments from Naaman in return for communicating spiritual grace to him. For Gehazi and all his offspring were struck with leprosy by God. Thus also, when the evangelical light first arose in the East, Ananias and Sapphira, for one lie alone — in that they concealed a certain part of their possessions — were suddenly deprived of this light, as recorded in Acts, chapter 5.

This reason is given by the ordinary gloss in these words: "the beginnings of laws are always commended by punishments: the two sons of Aaron, offering strange fire, are consumed. Oza, supporting the ark, fell dead. He fell so that other transgressors might be terrified by his example. It was not the cause of gain that gave so severe a sentence of transgression: but one who foresaw the future tares that would corrupt the simplicity of the Church with depraved morals — seeing that no penitence could cure the guilty — cut out the harmful root in order to instill fear in posterity" and so forth. And the interlinear gloss: "at the beginning, this swift punishment instilled great fear."

Certainly, greater abuses of simony and greater sins and unspeakable crimes were afterwards committed by human beings of the kinds mentioned above — yet God never punished them so severely. This matter is treated by the Master of Histories on Acts, chapter 22 of his History, and by Tostado on Joshua, chapter 7, question 59.
